speaker: interviewer
text: --in this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: In November of 1939. She came after the war, after Germany had invaded Poland. She came on an Italian liner. She came via Trieste and arrived in New York 10 or 11 days later. She looked terrible. She looked frail and sick. And she was very upset about my father's death. She felt responsible for it. She told me she felt responsible for it because she had introduced him to the Englishman who was accused of being a spy for England. And because of whom the Gestapo came to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were living in Chicago at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, I had just-- not officially, no. I was still living officially in Trenton, Elkins Park, wherever it may be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what plans were there for your mother's settlement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we rented an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Harper Avenue. 5454 South Harper Avenue.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago, for which my sister and I would share the rent. And I think I paid the rent and my sister gave $10 a week for food. And that's how we split up the support. And my mother was very frugal. And she adjusted to her new condition remarkably well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your mother been able to bring household possessions with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She brought nothing. But my grandfather's loft, which came from Berlin, did arrive, while our loft-- which is an English word for crate-- did not arrive. And everything had been crated by us, by my mother and father, including the paintings we had. Among the most famous was a portrait, which Lovis Corinth had painted of his wife. And this painting disappeared. The chauffeur had seen it in the house the day after my mother had gone to Berlin. And a couple of days later, when he came to the house again, the crate was missing. But, of course, nobody goes to the police and questions anybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But that painting disappeared. But, strangely enough, it wound up in a museum in Gorlitz in Germany. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when Mrs. Corinth compiled a list of all her husband's works, she assumed that this painting had been given to us-- she must have assumed it had been given by us to a museum, because in her book she says, this picture is owned by Dr. Otto Ollendorff and hangs in the museum in Gorlitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was in eastern--

speaker: interviewee
text: In eastern Germany. And we are trying to get that painting back. But the city of Gorlitz wants all kinds of documentation that that picture had actually been paid for by the Ollendorffs when they bought it in 1915, or whenever it was, and little things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know whether I'll ever get to see this painting again. But maybe my grandchildren will. So your mother arrived and had none of her household possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: None, none. She had a nightgown and some clothes. She came with one little suitcase and one a little bigger. All she could carry. See, she was in terrible condition when she left Berlin and was accompanied by a distant relative of hers, a second cousin, up to the Brenner Pass. But he couldn't go farther because he couldn't leave Germany. He had no exit permit. And so then my mother was alone on the train from the Brenner Pass to Trieste. Where the first night, I think, she stayed in a flop house near the harbor, which was cheap enough that she could pay for it with a few coins she had in her possession. Once she got on the ship, of course, the passage had been prepaid by us. And she got food on the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klaus, you had mentioned that the original papers had covered passage for your mother, your father, and your grandfather. And then her circumstance had changed. And so you were in square one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My grandfather was stuck. And the only reason he got out was when a Bible salesman--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did he get out finally?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940, in December. So he stayed in Germany another year. And a miracle occurred.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Bible salesman stopped at my aunt's house-- my grandfather's daughter's house-- and tried to sell her a Bible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Chicago, on Harper Avenue. And my aunt said to the Bible salesman, I don't need a Bible and no Bible can help me anyhow. The Bible salesman said, why? And she said, because I have a father who is trapped in Germany, who can't get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, give me your father's name. Maybe I can help you. And my aunt said, well, if you are a man of miracles, I surely will give you the name. How do you think you can help me? And he said, my minister from the Episcopalian Church, I think it was, is now the Chaplain of the Senate. And maybe he could run some interference for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a few days later my aunt got a telephone call that she should show up in Cordell Hull's office to discuss her plight with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Cordell Hull was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cordell Hull was the Secretary of State. And my aunt sent me. And so it was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did she send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I spoke better English, and I was east already anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were a young man and going to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the State Department. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what that was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, in retrospect, absolutely overwhelming. At the moment I probably didn't realize how important it was. But I remembered Mr. Hull, he looked very dignified. He stood up when I came in. We shook hands. And he beckoned me to sit down in a chair, asked me a few questions. I gave him all the answers. And then he stood up, and he says, you will hear from me if anything can be done. And I don't know whether he shook hands with me again. But I left. The whole thing took less than five minutes. And a week later, the State Department called at my aunt's house and said she should notify my grandfather in Berlin that he should report to the American consulate, where he will be issued a visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that all happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That all happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went, and he was given a visa in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berlin. In Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: A visa to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how did he arrange his passage? And what was that trip like?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was paid here. It was prepaid here. I don't know how the trip was. I mean, he was a very strong man. He never spoke much about trivia. I am sorry now that I didn't ask him how the passage was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he traveled through what other country to come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Italy. He came from Genoa to the States. My mother came from Trieste and he came from Genoa. And it was in the nick of time because a little later, you know, Italy was in the war too. And he couldn't have gone through Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he arrived at the end of 1940 then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you greet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --In America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We picked him up at the pier with my aunt. And then we drove through West Virginia and Washington. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think we stopped in Washington and thanked the Chaplain of the Senate. But that is too hazy now in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your grandfather then took up residence in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago. But then two years later, three years later, two and a half years later, moved to California. Because the climate-- he couldn't go out in winter or in summer. And so my aunt had bought a little house in Los Angeles, a tiny little bungalow. And he lived there 10 more years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had your grandfather's business been in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had small department stores and chain stores. Like similar to Woolworth's where the small stores. And similar to the 12th Street Store here in Chicago, which doesn't exist anymore, or Klein's Department Store, or Klaus' Department Store on Milwaukee Avenue. These were the bigger stores. And he had these stores in many towns all over Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they called?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were called [INAUDIBLE], or [INAUDIBLE], or [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] M. Konitzer und Sohn, M. Konitzer and Sons. The department stores were named Konitzer and the five and ten cent stores had initials.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did your grandfather live, then, in California?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in 1950, the year my youngest son was born. And initially [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my youngest son was called Thomas Alexander after him. But then a little bit later my wife changed the middle name to Andrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klaus, when Germany and the United States were at war, how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was of military age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 4F.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of my eyesight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to be in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I then tried the OWI. That was the Office of War Information. And I was at the verge of being accepted when VE Day came. These were also interesting interviews there, but not relevant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Earlier in your experience in the United States, just after the war began, for instance, did you have any anti-German feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anti-Nazi feeling, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Against you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Against who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Against you. You spoke with an accent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, here. Yes, I had an experience once when I was tutoring at Princeton. A friend of mine took me to the German Club there. And it was his first visit too. And we were there. And then when the president of the club came in, he held out his arm and said, heil Hitler. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this friend of mine decided that he doesn't want to go to those meetings anymore. And he said, let's leave. And we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was not you who said let's leave? What was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: He said that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your response?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was paralyzed. I don't know. I don't think I would have left. I would have sat there and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] listened it out and not go the second time.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your professional life, have you experienced anti-Semitism in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not overt. I know it exists. But my business associates are all Catholic. And we have the most harmonious coexistence imaginable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe how you started your business. How you met your wife. You have two sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I met my wife in 1939 at International House, where I was visiting. I didn't live there yet. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she was a student. She majored in French. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Lucille Halperin. And the Halperin family consisted of her and a brother, who became a great war hero. He was one of the first troops to land in Normandy, the night before. He also got the Navy Cross and all kinds of honors.

speaker: interviewee
text: The younger brother, who was killed in the war, after the war, in an airplane crash right after the end of the war in the Pacific. And two sisters, one who is 92, Mrs. [? Salinski, ?] who lives in Chicago at a retirement home, and another sister who lives in Minneapolis, Mrs. [? Malomed. ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What else? You met your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you are fall in love?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years later. We got married in '45. It was a stormy relationship, an on-again, off-again relationship. But one day in '45 she called me. She says, do you still want to marry me? And I said, yes. And so we got married at Hilton Chapel at the University of Chicago. We were married by a professor who taught comparative religion, who I believe was a Unitarian. Used to-- oh, no. He was the head of the ethical society.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you established yourself in business at that point? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was in some other business at the time. And I didn't want to leave town, which I would have had to do to stay in that other business. I started an electrical mail order business, which my father-in-law was very generous in supporting. In fact, by the time he got through supporting me, he had lent me $29,000, which was a lot of money. It was over a period of years. But I'm happy to say I repaid it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you start an electrical mail order business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody suggested it to me. If you can get merchandise-- it was 1945-- you can sell anything. And I was lucky enough to get some merchandise. And I had a friend by the name of Fred Ascher, who wrote for me a little flyer sheet. And I used a mailing company to mail it. It was R.L. Polk. And it was very successful. And it stayed successful for two years, almost two years. But then when general merchandise became available to everybody, it died. That business died, the mail order business. And then I went into the lighting maintenance business with my father-in-law. He was in the electric contracting business. And he allowed me to use his office facilities. And that's how I got started. And then I formed a relationship with this former engineer from that company, this Catholic friend. And we bought a building together. And we stayed together for 25 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: In business? As business partners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as partners. He had a company called Midwest Interstate. And I had Planned Lighting. But the business showed both names on the building. And we co-owned the building. And we were very helpful to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your wife involved in your business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My first wife and I got divorced on May 8, 1962.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that point you had how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two children. Tommy was born on May 23, 1950. And Daniel, the older one, was born on June 13, 1947. Both at Lying-in Hospital in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're divorced, but I know that today we will be joined later on by your wife, Kate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I met my new wife, Kate, through my business. Because at Christmas time I usually sent out little gifts to our customers, which were sent directly from the factory. And in 1962 that gift was a radio made by Sampson. And so I received a thank you note, which read, 'Note from Delilah regarding her new Sampson. I'm not just delighted, enchanted, surprised, but battery powered and transistorized. The cowboy, comedian, gangster, and Dick, are mine with a various delicate flick. A twist and it's Adlai before the UN. A turn and I'm riding a rocket with Glenn. A  touch, and it's Jackie declaiming an out. A spin and the Derby is ready to start. I'm up on my politics, boxing, and Greek. I'm savvy on books. And I am musically chic. I'm wiser, I'm deeper because of this box. And God knows I'm already too into the Sox.' And when I got that poem, I had the feeling that this is a person I would like to marry. But it took years to persuade her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: '62 To '66. Four years. And the night before we got married, I received in the mail a poem. I will not read every verse. But it starts out, 'Dear Klaus, I don't believe in rabbit's feet. I don't believe in mystics. But marriages can win or lose, depending on statistics. Eight is a size for dresses, unless a lady grows. 6 1/2 for gloves and mitts, 9 1/2 for hose. Reds and greens are my colors. Brown and pink I abhor. Ruffles and bows are avoided. Earrings and scent adored. Minks are OK for models. Jaguars are fine on cats. Diamonds are nice on others. Same can be said for hats. Small is the size for parties. Sleep is a breath of life. Noise is my great neurosis. Hobby is being wife. With love from Kate, Nearly Ollendorff.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went through with the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's wonderful. We'll be able to look at some of your photographs, get some more of your insights and philosophies, and meet Kate in a few minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you very much for your participation to this point. Thank you very much. You were very patient with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Klaus.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the house in which I was born, and in which I lived until I left for the United States. It was on the [GERMAN] in Breslau. This is a painting by Lovis Corinth, which had been in our house in Breslau ever since I lived there. It is a painting of his wife. This is from the index of Charlotte Corinth's book, where she compiled a list of all of her husband's paintings. As you can see, the owner is shown as Dr. Ollendorff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the painting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is And this picture has been exhibited in many museums, as is indicated below.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father acquired it when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle of the war and gave it to my mother as a wedding present.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which war?

speaker: interviewee
text: World War I. This is a picture of my uncle, Dr. Arthur Ollendorff. It was taken in our garden in Breslau in 1929. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1928. On the left is I, Klaus Ollendorff. Next is Frau Gustel, my governess. Next is my cousin Dorothy. And on the far right is my sister Ursula. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my father, Dr. Otto Ollendorff, who also had an honorary post as a Master and Chancery. Here he is shown in his judge's robes in approximately 1931. This picture was taken in 1930 in front of a hotel in the Sudeten Mountains. It was either in [INAUDIBLE], at the [INAUDIBLE] or in Johanessbad in front of some hotel. On the left is I, then my sister, then my mother. And on the right, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Mater Ollendorff. My father's name is Dr. Otto Ollendorff. My sister's name was then Ursula Ollendorff. It is now Ursula  Limner. And my name then was Klaus Ollendorff. And I have not changed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you were standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Klaus Ollendorff in approximately 1932, when I had been picked by my music teacher at the gymnasium to be in the children's chorus choir of the opera by Bizet. I appeared in that opera throughout the season. And there were two scenes in which I was. The first one I remember well. [SINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our winter garden in Breslau. This is my passport, which I used for my emigration from Germany and my immigration into the United States. This picture was taken shortly before issuance of this passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1935. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Berlin in 1936. Standing from the left, Dorothy Hermann, my cousin, Dr. Cohen's daughter, my father, Dr. Otto Ollendorff, my sister, Ursula Ollendorff, now Limner. Sitting on the left, Dr. Max Cohen. Next to my aunt, Erna Cohen, with her granddaughter, Michelle Hermann. Next my grandfather, Alex Konitzer. And next, my mother, Mater Ollendorff. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1948 in Los Angeles. On the left is my ex mother-in-law, Julia Halperin. Then my grandfather, Alexander Konitzer. Then my aunt, Erna Cohen. And on the far right, my ex-father-in-law Aaron Halperin. The Halperins gave the affidavits for my grandfather and for my mother. This is my son, Tommy. He doesn't want to be called Tommy. He's Tom. And it was taken in about 1978 in Ossining, New York. This picture was taken nearly 10 years ago at Henry [INAUDIBLE] birthday party at the Tavern Club. On the left is our grandson, Adam, then the grandmother, Kate, then Klaus Daniel, then Sherry, our daughter-in-law, then Jessica, our granddaughter, and Klaus Ollendorff, I. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These are our grandchildren, Adam and Jessica Ollendorff. And it was taken when Adam was 18 and Jessica 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1997. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my wife Kate, nee Rosenthal, who was born in Highland Park, Illinois.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Kate is very active in the Institute for Learning and Retirement at Northwestern University. She dedicates an enormous amount of time to this cause.

speaker: interviewee
text: So much that I've even gotten Klaus involved in it. He goes to a couple of courses there and, I think, blames me for it. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I also give you credit for many things. And I used to be so involved in many other organizations, like the Southeast Chicago Commission, the IVI, the Chicago Youth Centers, the board of the Jewish Vocational Service, and some others.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] campaign.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [INAUDIBLE] campaign and [INAUDIBLE] campaign, that I had hardly any time for myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to do some one upsmanship. I worked for Adlai Stevenson's presidential campaign for a whole year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, come here. May I touch you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your politics are very different from your father's it sounds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father was to the right of center and always voted that way. And I always leaned to the left. I hated the communists because of the Stalinists. Originally I thought their theory might actually be workable. But once I realized what happened in Spain during the Spanish Civil War, I became very active in anti-Stalinist movements.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very similar background, as a matter of fact. My father was very conservative and thought the world had come to an end because of Franklin Roosevelt, and labor unions, and such things. And I've been a devout liberal the rest of my life after growing up in that household. It's very strange what happens to people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kate, earlier Klaus read your thank-you note and another poem. How did you come to be on his gift list?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked as the administrative director of the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis. And we were customers of Planned Lighting, Klaus' business. He tended to the lights. And I believe we met in the library-- didn't we-- one day at the Institute. And I was scarcely aware that we had met. But he reminded me of it later. And I guess that's where it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but many customers got those gifts. I mean, you were not singled out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh you mean that I wrote a thank-you note.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mean, you were the only one who sent such a wonderful thank-you note.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, there's nothing like good manners.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when my secretary, Mrs. Haas, saw this note, she said to me in her nasal Philadelphia voice, marry her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why you did it. I never knew. I never knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klaus, do you have any words in closing? Any message for your--

speaker: interviewee
text: For my grandchildren, our grandchildren, I-- who are wonderful. I mean, better-- all grandchildren are wonderful. But ours are more wonderful. And my message to them is that they should be very happy and very grateful for having such wonderful parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you again for participating. And thank you, Kate, very much for joining--

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Klaus. This has been a perfect pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bye bye.

speaker: interviewee
text: Goodbye.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cook. [LAUGHS]
